title: What Is a Contradiction? A Framing Piece
subtitle: A shared vocabulary for logical contradictions, textual variants, harmonizable difficulties, and matters of interpretation — before the next five articles take up specific claimed difficulties
summary: Before examining any specific claimed difficulty between the Bible and the Quran, it helps to agree on vocabulary. This article distinguishes a genuine logical contradiction from a textual variant, a harmonizable difficulty, and a matter of interpretation, testing the distinctions on a real, widely discussed case — 2 Samuel 24:1 and 1 Chronicles 21:1 on who incited David's census — and on the Quran's own claim in 4:82 to contain no contradiction. It sets up the method the next five articles in this series will use.

seo.metaTitle: What Is a Contradiction? A Framing Piece | The Straight Path
seo.metaDescription: Learn to tell a genuine logical contradiction from a textual variant, a harmonizable difficulty, and a matter of interpretation, using 2 Samuel 24:1, 1 Chronicles 21:1, and Quran 4:82.

sections.title: In Brief
sections.body:
Before we look at any specific claimed difficulty between the Bible and the Quran — or within either book on its own — it helps to agree on words. "Contradiction" gets used loosely in both Muslim dawah literature and Christian apologetics, thrown at the other side's scripture like a verdict rather than a starting point for careful reading.

This article defines four different things people usually mean when they say a text "contradicts itself": a genuine logical contradiction (the same claim asserted and denied, in the same sense, at the same time), a textual variant (manuscripts of the same passage disagreeing on wording), a harmonizable difficulty (two true accounts, told from different angles, that can reasonably be read together), and a matter of interpretation (the wording is settled, but its meaning is debated).

We test this vocabulary on a real, widely discussed case: 2 Samuel 24:1 says the LORD moved David to take a census of Israel; 1 Chronicles 21:1 says Satan did it. Bible scholars classify this differently — some resolve it through a pattern of divine permission seen elsewhere in the Bible, others read it as evidence of theological development between two authors writing centuries apart. We also look at Quran 4:82, where the Quran claims that if it had come from anyone other than Allah, it would contain much contradiction, and examine carefully what Muslim scholarship does and does not mean by that claim.

None of this settles any specific difficulty on its own. It gives us shared words, so the five articles that follow this one can each name precisely what kind of difficulty is on the table, and present both traditions' answers at full strength.

sections.title: Why This Word Needs Defining
sections.body:
Open almost any polemical page about Islam and Christianity and the word "contradiction" appears within the first paragraph. Christian apologists compile lists of alleged contradictions in the Quran; Muslim writers compile far longer lists of alleged contradictions in the Bible. Both genres are old — Christian responses to claimed Quranic contradictions and Muslim responses to claimed biblical ones have circulated in pamphlet, book, and now video form for well over a century — and both share a habit: the word "contradiction" gets applied to almost anything that surprises a reader on a first pass, whether or not it is a logical contradiction in the strict sense.

This matters because the word does real work. Calling something a contradiction is a strong claim — it says two things cannot both be true, in any sense, under any reading. That is a much stronger claim than "these two accounts describe the same event differently," or "manuscripts disagree on a word," or "scholars read this verse two ways." Collapsing all of these into one word makes an argument sound stronger than its actual evidence supports, on both sides of this debate.

The five articles that follow this one will each take up one specific, well-known claimed difficulty — the kind of material both dawah literature and Christian apologetics use as ammunition against the other tradition — and work through it fairly, stating the honest difficulty and presenting the real, strongest answers from both sides. Before we get there, this article does the housekeeping: it defines the four categories used throughout the series, and shows what disciplined classification actually looks like, using one case from the Bible and one from the Quran.

sections.title: The Question: What Do We Actually Mean by "Contradiction"?
sections.body:
Not every disagreement between two statements is a logical contradiction. Philosophy has a precise definition, going back to Aristotle's law of non-contradiction: a genuine contradiction exists when the same claim is both asserted and denied, about the same subject, in the same sense, at the same time. "The car is red" and "the car is not red," said of the same car at the same moment, is a contradiction. "The car was red in 2020" and "the car is blue in 2024" is not — it is a claim about change over time. Most disagreements people label "contradictions" in religious texts do not actually meet this strict definition once examined closely, which is exactly why careful classification matters.

A textual variant is a different thing altogether: it is a disagreement between manuscripts, or between manuscript families or reading traditions, over the wording of the same passage in the same document. It is not a claim about what happened in history or what is theologically true — it is a question in the field of textual criticism, about which surviving witness (or combination of witnesses) best preserves what was originally written. The well-known Christian-side example is the last twelve verses of the Gospel of Mark (16:9-20): they appear in the majority of later Greek manuscripts but are absent from the two oldest and most respected manuscripts, Codex Sinaiticus and Codex Vaticanus, which is why modern translations typically flag them with a footnote. On the Quranic side, the handful of authenticated qira'at — reading traditions such as Hafs and Warsh, each transmitted through unbroken chains back to the Prophet ﷺ — differ on some points of pronunciation and occasionally a word, without Islamic scholarship treating this as rival originals competing for the same text; the tradition holds that multiple authentic modes of recitation were themselves taught and permitted from the beginning. Either way, a textual variant is a transmission-history question, not a truth-claim contradiction.

A harmonizable difficulty is different again: it is a case where two accounts, both aiming to report something true, are told from different angles, for different purposes, or with different emphasis and compression, such that a single coherent scenario can reasonably be reconstructed behind both, even if its exact original shape cannot be recovered with total certainty. The two accounts are not mutually exclusive; they can both be true, describing the same reality from different vantage points, the way two honest witnesses to a car accident might each report accurately on what they personally saw without either one lying.

A matter of interpretation is different still: here the wording is not disputed (no variant), and there is no need to reconcile two separate texts (not a harmonization question) — instead, competent, honest readers disagree about what one stable text actually means. Is a phrase figurative or literal? Is a narrative arranged chronologically or by theme? These are ordinary questions of exegesis, the kind serious readers argue about in any ancient text, religious or secular.

These four categories sometimes blur at the edges, and serious scholars can disagree about which one applies to a given case. That disagreement is not, by itself, evidence of bad faith on anyone's part — it is what careful engagement with ancient religious texts actually looks like.

sections.title: A Textbook Case: Who Incited David's Census?
sections.body:
One case appears constantly in this discussion, on both skeptical "list of Bible contradictions" websites and in serious academic commentaries, because it is unusually clean: one short clause, one act, two different named agents.

In 2 Samuel 24, David orders a census of Israel and Judah, an act the narrative treats as sinful, followed by a plague God sends against Israel as judgment. The chapter opens: "Again Yahweh’s anger burned against Israel, and he moved David against them, saying, “Go, count Israel and Judah.”" (2 Samuel 24:1, World English Bible). In 1 Chronicles 21, the Hebrew Bible's other, later retelling of the same episode, opens with a different subject for the same verb: "Satan stood up against Israel, and moved David to take a census of Israel." (1 Chronicles 21:1, World English Bible).

A translation note matters here: the World English Bible and the King James Version both render the underlying Hebrew verb (from the root sut, "to incite" or "entice") as "moved" in both verses, while other widely used translations such as the NIV, ESV, and NASB render the same verb "incited" in both places. Different English words, same Hebrew verb, same structure in both books — which is precisely why the identity of the subject performing that action is the whole question.

The two books are not the same document copied twice. Samuel-Kings belongs to the sweeping history scholars usually call the Deuteronomistic History; most agree it reached something close to its present form during or shortly after the Babylonian exile, in the sixth century BC, though it draws on older sources. Chronicles is a separate, later composition, generally dated to the Persian period, roughly the fourth century BC, written for the restored post-exilic community and covering much of the same history with its own selection, omissions, additions, and theological emphases — the Chronicler regularly retells episodes from Samuel-Kings with noticeable changes in wording and focus. The census story is one of the clearest examples, because the change here is not a minor detail but the identity of the verse's subject: who, exactly, incited David.

This pairing shows up in Gleason Archer's Encyclopedia of Bible Difficulties, a standard evangelical harmonization reference, and equally in critical, historical commentaries such as Sara Japhet's work on Chronicles. That range tells you something useful on its own: this is not a case invented by hostile critics that believers have scrambled to explain away after the fact. It is a genuine, long-recognized feature of the text that serious readers across the theological spectrum have discussed, in their own terms, for a very long time.

sections.title: The Quran's Own Claim: Quran 4:82
sections.body:
أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ ۚ وَلَوْ كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ غَيْرِ ٱللَّهِ لَوَجَدُوا۟ فِيهِ ٱخْتِلَٰفًۭا كَثِيرًۭا

"Then do they not reflect upon the Qur'an? If it had been from [any] other than Allah, they would have found within it much contradiction." (Quran 4:82, Saheeh International)

This verse sits within a passage of Surah An-Nisa addressing believers wavering under real pressure — social, political, and at times military — in Medina. Read in context, it functions as an invitation to scrutiny rather than a slogan: reflect (tadabbur) on this text carefully, and see for yourself whether it holds together.

Classical Muslim exegesis, from al-Tabari onward, reads the underlying argument like this: the Quran was revealed across roughly twenty-three years, addressing a community whose circumstances changed dramatically — years of persecution in Mecca, the establishment of a new community in Medina, war, peace, victory, loss, and a Prophet ﷺ whose own life circumstances shifted enormously across that span. A human author composing a text under those changing conditions over that much time would, on ordinary human experience, be expected to show drift: shifting positions, forgotten commitments, tensions between earlier and later material reflecting a change of mind or circumstance. The claim of 4:82 is that sustained, careful reflection on the Quran's actual content — its legal rulings, its narratives, its theological claims — does not turn up that kind of internal drift. In classical Islamic scholarship this is presented as one argument among several for the Quran's divine origin, alongside the central argument from linguistic inimitability (i'jaz al-Qur'an); together they form a genre of Islamic apologetics defending the Quran's uniqueness that developed across centuries, from scholars such as al-Baqillani onward.

It is worth being precise about what this verse claims and what it does not. It is not a claim that no verse will ever look surprising to a reader encountering two passages side by side without training — Islamic scholarship developed an entire apparatus specifically because apparent tensions do arise and require real interpretive work: asbab al-nuzul (the occasions of revelation, explaining why an earlier verse addresses one situation and a later verse a changed one), naskh (the acknowledged abrogation of some earlier legal rulings by later ones, understood as a feature of law revealed in stages to a community whose circumstances were changing, not one verse falsifying another), and the Quran's own distinction, drawn in Quran 3:7, between muhkam verses (clear in their meaning) and mutashabih verses (whose fuller sense requires interpretive effort). An entire classical genre, mushkil al-Qur'an ("the problematic [passages] of the Qur'an"), exists specifically to work through passages that appear, on a surface reading, to sit in tension with one another.

Nor is 4:82 a claim that settles questions outside the Quran's own text. It is a claim about the Quran's internal coherence — not a verdict on how to reconcile an outside historical report, not an answer to a specific claimed discrepancy between the Quran and the Bible, and not a resolution of questions historians raise about the Quran's compilation and transmission. Those are each separate scholarly conversations, argued on their own evidence. Muslim scholarship does not treat 4:82 as pre-empting them, and this article does not use it that way either.

sections.title: How Christian Scholarship Reads the Census Narrative: Two Real Answers
sections.body:
"Again Yahweh’s anger burned against Israel, and he moved David against them, saying, “Go, count Israel and Judah.”" (2 Samuel 24:1, World English Bible)

"Satan stood up against Israel, and moved David to take a census of Israel." (1 Chronicles 21:1, World English Bible)

Committed Christian scholars do not all classify this pairing the same way, and that disagreement is worth presenting honestly rather than flattening into one official answer.

The harmonization reading, common among scholars holding a high view of Scripture's inerrancy, treats this as a harmonizable difficulty resolved by a pattern found elsewhere in the Bible: dual causation, or what theologians call primary and secondary causality. On this reading, Satan is the proximate agent, the one who actively tempts David toward the sinful act of the census, while God is the ultimate agent, in the sense that nothing occurs outside His sovereign permission, and His anger against Israel, mentioned explicitly in 2 Samuel 24:1 as the reason a census became the occasion of judgment, stands behind Satan's action rather than replacing it. This is not treated as an isolated move invented solely to rescue this one passage: the same pattern appears in Job 1-2, where Satan afflicts Job only within limits God permits; in 1 Kings 22:19-23, where a spirit volunteers to become a "lying spirit" in the mouths of false prophets and the LORD sends it to accomplish His judgment; and in Genesis 50:20, where Joseph tells his brothers that what they intended for evil, God intended for good, in the very same act. On this view, "the LORD... moved David" and "Satan... moved David" describe two real agents acting at two different levels of causation in the same event, which is not a strict logical contradiction (a claim asserted and denied) but a theological account of how divine sovereignty and creaturely, even malevolent, agency work together, an account Christian theology has developed technical vocabulary for over centuries (compatibilism, concurrence).

A second reading, common among scholars using historical-critical method, including many who are themselves Christian and hold Scripture to be inspired without applying a strict harmonizing approach to every historical detail, reads the difference as evidence of real theological development between the two books' composition. On this view, the Chronicler, writing generations later for a post-exilic community shaped by a period in which Jewish thought was increasingly working out how a good, sovereign God relates to evil and temptation, revised the wording inherited from Samuel to introduce an intermediary rather than attribute David's incitement to sin directly to God. This reading is sometimes bolstered by a grammatical detail: in Job 1-2 and Zechariah 3, the Hebrew carries the definite article, ha-satan ("the satan," more a title, the accuser or adversary, than a personal name), while 1 Chronicles 21:1 lacks the article, reading simply satan. Most translations and commentators, ancient and modern, still take it as referring to a personal supernatural adversary given the context, but a minority of scholars have argued this grammatical difference leaves room to read the term more generically. On the developmental reading, this passage is less a contradiction to be logically dissolved than a window into how a later biblical author reworked earlier material as the community's theological reflection matured, which these scholars regard as compatible with, not a threat to, the text's inspiration and value.

Both readings agree on something important: this is not a case of one verse asserting a claim and another flatly denying that same claim. Both books agree a census happened, that it was sinful, and that judgment followed. What is genuinely at issue is which classification best explains a real difference in how the two books identify the inciting agent — a harmonizable difficulty resting on a well-attested biblical pattern, or a theologically significant editorial development across centuries. Readers of this library will meet both kinds of answers across the specific-difficulty articles that follow, and both deserve to be represented at full strength rather than dismissed.

sections.title: Applying the Vocabulary
sections.body:
Run the four categories over both cases above and the shape of each becomes easier to see.

The census narrative is not a genuine logical contradiction in the strict sense: nowhere does either text assert a claim and also deny that same claim in the same sense. It is also not a textual variant: both wordings are stable across the entire manuscript tradition of their respective books; this is a difference between two separate authors and books, not between competing manuscript copies of one passage. That leaves the two live options actually debated by scholars: a harmonizable difficulty, if one agent acts under the sovereign permission of the other, a pattern attested independently elsewhere in the Bible, or a matter of theological development, if the Chronicler is understood to have deliberately reworked inherited material to reflect a later theological sensibility. Both are serious, real classifications; neither is "contradiction" in the strict sense, which is exactly why this case is such a useful teaching example.

Quran 4:82 makes an internal-coherence claim of a similar shape: it holds that the Quran's own text, examined carefully, will not be found to contain the kind of drift a single human author writing across changing circumstances over decades would be expected to produce. When later Muslim scholars encountered a specific verse pair that looked, on first reading, to sit in tension, they did not treat 4:82 as forbidding the question. They developed working tools, asbab al-nuzul, naskh, the muhkam and mutashabih distinction, and the broader mushkil al-Qur'an literature, to classify and resolve the specific case, much as biblical scholars developed the vocabulary of dual causation, source criticism, and redaction to work through cases like the census narrative.

Both traditions, in other words, have long-standing internal disciplines for exactly this kind of question — the Christian tradition through harmonies of the Gospels going back to Tatian's second-century Diatessaron and Augustine's Harmony of the Gospels, and the Islamic tradition through tafsir's treatment of mushkil and naskh. A fair reader owes both traditions the use of their own tools before reaching for the word "contradiction."

sections.title: Common Misconceptions About "Contradiction"
sections.body:
One common misconception is that a published list of Bible contradictions or Quran contradictions has already done the work of classification. In practice, most such lists, on both sides of this debate, conflate all four categories under one heading: a textual variant, a harmonizable difficulty, and a matter of interpretation are all counted as "contradictions" alongside any genuine logical contradictions that might actually appear, inflating the count and the rhetorical force of the list without inflating its actual evidential weight.

A second misconception runs the other way: that calling something a harmonizable difficulty instead of a contradiction is automatically a face-saving move invented after the fact. Sometimes a proposed harmonization is weak and unconvincing. But sometimes, as with the dual-causation reading of the census narrative, the harmonization draws on a pattern independently attested elsewhere in the same corpus, in Job 1-2, 1 Kings 22, and Genesis 50:20, rather than being invented solely to solve this one passage, which is a meaningfully stronger kind of argument than an isolated ad hoc rescue, and deserves to be weighed as such rather than dismissed by category alone.

A third misconception is that Quran 4:82's claim means Muslim scholars never discuss any apparent tension within the Quranic text. The opposite is true: entire classical genres, such as Ibn Qutayba's work resolving apparently conflicting hadith reports and the broader mushkil al-Qur'an literature addressing apparently difficult Quranic passages, exist because trained scholars took such questions seriously enough to write extensively about them.

A fourth misconception is that landing on "matter of interpretation" is a weaker or evasive result compared to identifying a hard contradiction. Interpretive disagreement over a stable, undisputed text is the normal condition of reading any ancient document closely — legal historians argue about the meaning of settled statutes, literary scholars argue about the meaning of settled poems — and none of that implies a defect in the text itself.

sections.title: Both Traditions, on Their Own Terms
sections.body:
This vocabulary does not resolve a single specific difficulty on its own, and it is not meant to. Its job is narrower and more useful: when the next five articles in this series each take up one specific, well-known claimed difficulty, the kind of material both Muslim dawah literature and Christian apologetics use as ammunition against the other tradition, we can say precisely what kind of difficulty is actually on the table, instead of reaching for the word "contradiction" as a blunt instrument.

A genuine logical contradiction, the same claim asserted and denied, in the same sense, at the same time, is a serious and different thing from a textual variant, manuscripts disagreeing on wording, a harmonizable difficulty, two true accounts read together from different angles, or a matter of interpretation, a stable text whose meaning is debated. The census narrative in 2 Samuel 24:1 and 1 Chronicles 21:1 shows two live, serious classifications actually held by real Bible scholars, a harmonizable difficulty resting on a genuine biblical pattern of dual causation, or a marker of theological development across centuries of composition, and neither is the strict logical contradiction the word often implies in casual use. Quran 4:82's claim that the Quran contains no contradiction is, in Muslim scholarship's own understanding, a claim about the Quran's internal coherence under sustained reflection, worked out using the tradition's own tools, asbab al-nuzul, naskh, muhkam and mutashabih, not a claim that forecloses careful examination of specific passages, and not a claim about matters outside the Quran's own text.

Each tradition has developed serious, centuries-old internal disciplines for handling exactly this kind of question. The fair way to read either tradition's response to a claimed difficulty is through those internal tools, in their own terms, not through a caricature that treats every tension as either a devastating verdict or a problem too embarrassing to name. The five articles that follow will each try to do exactly that: name the honest difficulty at full strength, then set out how Christian scholarship and Islamic scholarship each actually address it, without declaring either tradition refuted.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Is every difference between two accounts of the same event a contradiction?
A: No. A contradiction requires the same claim to be asserted and denied, in the same sense, at the same time. Two accounts that describe the same real event from different angles, with different emphasis or compression, can both be true — that is a harmonizable difficulty, not a contradiction.

Q: What is the difference between a textual variant and a contradiction?
A: A textual variant is a disagreement between manuscripts, or reading traditions, over the wording of the same passage — a question about transmission history, like the debated ending of Mark's Gospel or differences among the Quran's authenticated qira'at. A contradiction is a claim about truth: that two assertions cannot both be true in any sense. They are different kinds of question entirely.

Q: Doesn't calling something "harmonizable" just mean scholars are making excuses?
A: Sometimes a harmonization is weak. But a harmonization that draws on a pattern independently attested elsewhere in the same text, such as the Bible's recurring pattern of God permitting a lesser agent to act toward a sovereign purpose, seen in Job, 1 Kings 22, and Genesis 50, is a stronger, more principled argument than an isolated after-the-fact rescue, and should be weighed on its own merits.

Q: What does Quran 4:82 actually claim?
A: That the Quran's own text, examined carefully, does not contain the kind of internal drift a single human author would be expected to produce writing across changing circumstances over twenty-three years. Muslim scholarship reads it as a claim about the Quran's internal coherence, not as a claim that no verse will ever look surprising on first reading, and not as a verdict on questions outside the Quran's own text.

Q: Why do 2 Samuel 24:1 and 1 Chronicles 21:1 come up so often in this discussion?
A: Because the case is unusually clean — one short clause, one act (inciting David's census), two different named agents (the LORD and Satan). That compactness makes it a favorite teaching example on both skeptical and apologetic sides, and a genuinely useful test case for the vocabulary this article sets out.

Q: What should I expect from the next five articles in this series?
A: Each will take up one specific, well-known claimed difficulty between Islam and Christianity, state it as strongly and honestly as its sharpest critics do, and then present the real, strongest responses from both Christian and Islamic scholarship, using the vocabulary defined here, without declaring either tradition refuted.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
These works illustrate the two approaches to the census narrative described above, and the depth of each tradition's own internal tools for handling apparent textual difficulty.

Gleason L. Archer — Encyclopedia of Bible Difficulties: a standard evangelical harmonization reference that works through the 2 Samuel 24:1 / 1 Chronicles 21:1 pairing alongside hundreds of similar cases, using the dual-causation approach described in this article.

Sara Japhet — I & II Chronicles: A Commentary (Old Testament Library): a leading critical commentary examining how the Chronicler reworked material inherited from Samuel-Kings, including the census narrative's shift in the identity of the inciting agent.

Bart D. Ehrman — Jesus, Interrupted: Revealing the Hidden Contradictions in the Bible: a widely read presentation of the historical-critical approach to differences among biblical books, representing the theological-development reading discussed in this article.

Ibn Qutayba — Ta'wil Mukhtalif al-Hadith: a classical ninth-century work devoted to systematically resolving apparently conflicting hadith reports, illustrating the depth of the Islamic tradition's own internal tools for handling textual difficulty.

quranVerses.arabic: أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ ۚ وَلَوْ كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ غَيْرِ ٱللَّهِ لَوَجَدُوا۟ فِيهِ ٱخْتِلَٰفًۭا كَثِيرًۭا
quranVerses.translation: Then do they not reflect upon the Qur'an? If it had been from [any] other than Allah, they would have found within it much contradiction.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ عَلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَٰبَ مِنْهُ ءَايَٰتٌۭ مُّحْكَمَٰتٌ هُنَّ أُمُّ ٱلْكِتَٰبِ وَأُخَرُ مُتَشَٰبِهَٰتٌۭ ۖ فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ زَيْغٌۭ فَيَتَّبِعُونَ مَا تَشَٰبَهَ مِنْهُ ٱبْتِغَآءَ ٱلْفِتْنَةِ وَٱبْتِغَآءَ تَأْوِيلِهِۦ ۗ وَمَا يَعْلَمُ تَأْوِيلَهُۥٓ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَٱلرَّٰسِخُونَ فِى ٱلْعِلْمِ يَقُولُونَ ءَامَنَّا بِهِۦ كُلٌّۭ مِّنْ عِندِ رَبِّنَا ۗ وَمَا يَذَّكَّرُ إِلَّآ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَلْبَٰبِ
quranVerses.translation: It is He who has sent down to you, [O Muhammad], the Book; in it are verses [that are] precise - they are the foundation of the Book - and others unspecific. As for those in whose hearts is deviation [from truth], they will follow that of it which is unspecific, seeking discord and seeking an interpretation [suitable to them]. And no one knows its [true] interpretation except Allah. But those firm in knowledge say, "We believe in it. All [of it] is from our Lord." And no one will be reminded except those of understanding.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

bibleVerses.text: Again Yahweh’s anger burned against Israel, and he moved David against them, saying, “Go, count Israel and Judah.”
Satan stood up against Israel, and moved David to take a census of Israel.

Micaiah said, “Therefore hear Yahweh’s word. I saw Yahweh sitting on his throne, and all the army of heaven standing by him on his right hand and on his left.
Yahweh said, ‘Who will entice Ahab, that he may go up and fall at Ramoth Gilead?’ One said one thing; and another said another.
A spirit came out and stood before Yahweh, and said, ‘I will entice him.’
Yahweh said to him, ‘How?’
He said, ‘I will go out and will be a lying spirit in the mouth of all his prophets.’
He said, ‘You will entice him, and will also prevail. Go out and do so.’
Now therefore, behold, Yahweh has put a lying spirit in the mouth of all these your prophets; and Yahweh has spoken evil concerning you.”
As for you, you meant evil against me, but God meant it for good, to bring to pass, as it is today, to save many people alive.

faq.question: Is every difference between two accounts of the same event a contradiction?
faq.answer: No. A contradiction requires the same claim to be asserted and denied, in the same sense, at the same time. Two accounts that describe the same real event from different angles, with different emphasis or compression, can both be true — that is a harmonizable difficulty, not a contradiction.

faq.question: What is the difference between a textual variant and a contradiction?
faq.answer: A textual variant is a disagreement between manuscripts, or reading traditions, over the wording of the same passage — a question about transmission history, like the debated ending of Mark's Gospel or differences among the Quran's authenticated qira'at. A contradiction is a claim about truth: that two assertions cannot both be true in any sense. They are different kinds of question entirely.

faq.answer: Sometimes a harmonization is weak. But a harmonization that draws on a pattern independently attested elsewhere in the same text, such as the Bible's recurring pattern of God permitting a lesser agent to act toward a sovereign purpose, seen in Job, 1 Kings 22, and Genesis 50, is a stronger, more principled argument than an isolated after-the-fact rescue, and should be weighed on its own merits.

faq.answer: That the Quran's own text, examined carefully, does not contain the kind of internal drift a single human author would be expected to produce writing across changing circumstances over twenty-three years. Muslim scholarship reads it as a claim about the Quran's internal coherence, not as a claim that no verse will ever look surprising on first reading, and not as a verdict on questions outside the Quran's own text.

faq.answer: Because the case is unusually clean — one short clause, one act (inciting David's census), two different named agents (the LORD and Satan). That compactness makes it a favorite teaching example on both skeptical and apologetic sides, and a genuinely useful test case for the vocabulary this article sets out.

faq.answer: Each will take up one specific, well-known claimed difficulty between Islam and Christianity, state it as strongly and honestly as its sharpest critics do, and then present the real, strongest responses from both Christian and Islamic scholarship, using the vocabulary defined here, without declaring either tradition refuted.

furtherReading.title: Encyclopedia of Bible Difficulties
furtherReading.author: Gleason L. Archer
furtherReading.note: A standard evangelical harmonization reference that works through the 2 Samuel 24:1 / 1 Chronicles 21:1 pairing alongside hundreds of similar cases, using the dual-causation approach described in this article.

furtherReading.title: I & II Chronicles: A Commentary (Old Testament Library)
furtherReading.author: Sara Japhet
furtherReading.note: A leading critical commentary examining how the Chronicler reworked material inherited from Samuel-Kings, including the census narrative's shift in the identity of the inciting agent.

furtherReading.title: Jesus, Interrupted: Revealing the Hidden Contradictions in the Bible
furtherReading.author: Bart D. Ehrman
furtherReading.note: A widely read presentation of the historical-critical approach to differences among biblical books, representing the theological-development reading discussed in this article.

furtherReading.title: Ta'wil Mukhtalif al-Hadith
furtherReading.author: Ibn Qutayba
furtherReading.note: A classical ninth-century work devoted to systematically resolving apparently conflicting hadith reports, illustrating the depth of the Islamic tradition's own internal tools for handling textual difficulty.
